content:
Member-only story

Spec-Driven Development has no fixed sign for Developer Experience. The same rollout lifts some teams and crushes others — four rollout decisions decide which.

11 min read
Just now

--

Everyone asks whether Spec-Driven Development is good or bad for developers — and that’s the wrong question.
On one team, under the exact same rollout, one senior engineer says, “I can finally think at a higher level,” and another says, “I’m a code-cranking machine that cleans up after AI.” Both are right. And that should worry anyone currently weighing SDD for their team.
I’ve watched this split up close several times, and it caught me off guard the same way every time. It was never about who was the better developer — both were excellent. It was about how the process had been rolled out around them.
Over the next ten minutes, I’ll break down exactly what in SDD pushes day-to-day experience toward flow or toward frustration. Instead of leaving you with a useless “it depends,” I’ll show you the four rollout decisions that settle it — and what they actually hinge on.
The Bottom Line If You Bail After Three…
